title: JBS Dev: On imperfect data and the AI last mile – from model capability to cost sustainability

raw_body:
Joe Rose, president at strategic technology provider JBS Dev, wants to cut through one of the myths of working with generative and agentic AI systems. “It’s a common misconception that your data has to be perfect before you do any of these types of workloads,” he explains. As a recent article in AI Fieldbook outlines, […]
The post JBS Dev: On imperfect data and the AI last mile – from model capability to cost sustainability appeared first on AI News.
speedrun: Joe Rose, president of JBS Dev, challenges the idea that perfect data is essential for using generative AI systems. He highlights that many organizations mistakenly believe they need flawless data before deploying these technologies. This misconception can hinder innovation and slow down progress in AI applications. Understanding that imperfect data can still yield valuable insights is crucial as businesses increasingly rely on AI.
why_it_matters: Organizations can start using AI tools sooner, which could lead to quicker innovations and solutions for specific challenges.
This perspective may shift how companies approach data collection, emphasizing agility over perfection in AI development.

lenses.eli12: Joe Rose from JBS Dev wants people to know that you don’t need perfect data to use AI effectively. Think of it like cooking; you can still make a good meal with imperfect ingredients. This understanding is important because it allows more people to explore AI without being held back by the fear of having 'perfect' data.
lenses.pm: For product managers, this insight means they can launch AI features without waiting for ideal data conditions. It opens up opportunities to gather user feedback and iterate on products more quickly. Emphasizing flexibility in data requirements could lead to more efficient development cycles.
lenses.engineer: From a technical standpoint, Joe Rose's argument suggests that AI models can still perform well even with suboptimal data. This could encourage engineers to experiment with real-world datasets rather than striving for unattainable perfection. It’s a shift that may foster more practical applications of AI technologies.
